text: The ball design is similar the Jabulani, which had big problems with movement. They supposedly added a lot of features to mitigate the Jabulani's tendency to knuckle easily. Could be that it's still an issue somewhat. There have definitely been a lot of "keeper really should have saved that, wtf" type goals.
label: analysis

text: Keep in mind that for every WC we have a new ball and the producers always introduce something new to it to showcase their products. The size and the weight is the same, but small details like the way you stich or glue it together have an impact on the way the ball moves. The Jabulani is probably the best example. The fact that the ball was too smooth, chaged the way it rotates at high speeds which affected the direction it goes. Keepers hated it, but fans loved it because it gave us a lot of bangers.
label: analysis

text: FIFA have specs as to what the ball has to be, but there is still wiggle room around that as long as you adhere to the specs. For instance weight, pressure, circumference. But things like panel design, stitching, material has a lot of wiggle room for what is allowed and these being different can alter the characteristics of how the ball performs. Look at the panel design or this ball for instance, Adidas have used large unusually designed panels that flow around the ball. If you look where the seems meet, something like this could effect wind resistance as the air travels across the surface of the ball when it's kicked - versus a traditional ball where the panels are equal all around the ball, meaning in theory it could be more stable as it flies through the air.
label: analysis

text:
Europeans subconsciously gatekeep soccer fandom when saying stuff like this. You guys do it all the time that it starts to reek of some sort of entrenched condescending colonialism or worse. They never think of the millions and millions of Latin Americans who watch their Latin American teams play. They don't acknowledge the people who follow teams that have little to do with Europe or the States. The country that literally championed "all day soccer" decades before Europe decided to get with the program? On any given weekend, there are up to 15 MLS matches available to US fans... and about 50 European matches, and those European matches happen all throughout the day, often with no-competing sports on at the same time.  Meanwhile, MLS stadia are healthily full, the league is the second-most attended in the world. And while they do have to compete with 4 firmly-entrenched American sports leagues, they are doing alright for themselves.
Also, there is no "the" in front of "MLS." When an acronym is primarily used as the main identifier, you don't use "the." For example: NATO. UNESCO.
label: analysis

text: I am really 50/50 on this head-to-head rule. It's just second matchday, and 2 teams have been eliminated. Previously they still would have chances till last matchday. Now there’s 2 dead matches in the last round. I understand why this rule was given preference over goal difference, however we are now looking at probably 3-4 more eliminations before 3rd matchday begins.
label: analysis

text: And it's not only the tactical adjustments teams get to make to break momentum. Soccer is also a game of attrition, it'sabout wearing down your opponents faster than yourself, managing your strength for 90 minutes is key skill. The extra breaks negate that to a large degree.
label: analysis

text: UEFA have already stated that they won't implement this mandatory "hydration break" in their competitions. If there's to be a break, it'll be a decision of the referee, it's got to be at least 32°C and it won't last more than 1 minute.
label: analysis

text: Apart from the fact that this stops the game for ad breaks, it also allows players to discuss strategy with their coach. We've seen people joke about having 4 quarters now, but that's actually what it is. If players restrategize with the coach then it effectively becomes a quarter. And it sucks.
label: analysis
